---
title: "3 · Physics — Part 2: Hydromechanics"
---

The mechanical state is the near-surface stiffness that geotechnical hazard models consume, and the
petrophysical link is how a change in water produces a measurable change in seismic velocity — through
an effective-medium relation with material parameters, not an empirical correlation.

## Mechanical modelling grounds

The mechanical state is the near-surface **shear-wave velocity** $V_S(z)$ and, through it, the
small-strain stiffness that geotechnical hazard models consume. It is constrained three ways, in
decreasing data cost. *Seismic tomography* from ambient noise inverts inter-station surface-wave
dispersion for $V_S(z)$ [@shapiro2005high] — the highest-fidelity but sparsest constraint. *Vs30
proxies* — the 30 m time-averaged velocity, $V_{S30} = 30 / \int_0^{30} dz/V_S(z)$ — trade fidelity
for coverage: the Wald–Allen topographic-slope model [@waldallen2007], which we adopt at native 90 m,
and geology- and geomorphon-based parametric models [@sanger2025parametric]. Vs30 maps to the
NEHRP/ASCE-7 site class, and because the proxy carries large residual scatter we propagate that as a
**site-class probability** rather than a point value (see [Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)).
Frequency sets depth: a Rayleigh wave of frequency $f$ is most sensitive near $L \approx V_S/3f$, so a
band of frequencies reads a profile of depths — the basis of the depth kernels (`codameter`/`disba`)
used by the $\delta v/v$ module. Bulk properties needed to turn velocity into stress — density and the
lithologic control on stiffness — come from the same SOLUS static set (oven-dry bulk density, texture)
and the geology/lithology layer that also feeds the storage envelope, so the mechanical and
hydrological grounds share one static description of the subsurface. This stiffness field is the input
to the Sanger liquefaction GLM [@sanger2025liquefaction] and, with the water table, to the LandLab
landslide chain ([Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)).

## Petrophysical link: velocity, stiffness, and water

The two grounds meet in petrophysics: **water changes stiffness, and $\delta v/v$ measures the
change** — not as an empirical correlation but through an effective-medium relation with measurable
material parameters [@denolle2026unified]. The observable is a depth-averaged, frequency-dependent
surface-wave phase-velocity change,

$$
\frac{\delta v}{v}(f,t) \approx \frac{\delta c}{c}(f,t)
= \frac{1}{c(f)}\int_0^{\infty} \frac{\partial c}{\partial V_S}(z,f)\,\frac{\delta V_S}{V_S}(z,t)\,V_S\,dz,
$$

so a band of frequencies reads a *profile* of $\delta V_S(z)$ (peak sensitivity near $L\approx V_S/3f$),
recovered through the Rayleigh kernels. What changes $V_S=\sqrt{\mu_{\rm eff}/\rho}$ is, to leading
order, the **effective shear modulus** $\mu_{\rm eff}$, not the density: a wetting-driven density change
contributes $\delta V_S/V_S = -\tfrac12\,\delta\rho/\rho$, two to three orders of magnitude smaller than
the modulus term for typical soils [@denolle2026unified]. The modulus is set by grain-contact mechanics
— Hertz–Mindlin gives $\mu_{\rm eff}\propto P_e^{1/3}$, so the pressure derivative $\mu'=d\mu/dP\sim\mu/P$
is very large at the low confining pressures of the near surface; this is why seasonal $\delta v/v$
exceeds $0.1\%$ in soft sediments but is below $0.01\%$ in crystalline rock, and why the signal lives in
the shallowest layers. The two hydrologic regimes, split at the water table, differ in *what sets the
effective pressure* $P_e$.

### Saturated zone — pore pressure (poroelastic + acoustoelastic)

Below the water table the effective stress is overburden minus pore pressure (Terzaghi/Biot
[@biot1941general]); a head change $\Delta h$ raises pore pressure by $\rho_w g\,\Delta h$, lowers
effective stress, and softens the frame. In the poroelastic surface-wave form the pore-pressure term
$u^0$ reduces velocity, $\left(\delta V_S/V_S\right)_{\rm Rayleigh} = -\tfrac{\mu'}{2\mu}u^0 +
\tfrac{\mu'+1}{12\mu}T^0_{33}$, and the underlying acoustoelastic (third-order-elastic) sensitivity
$\delta v/v = \beta\,\epsilon_{kk}$ is bridged to the seismological moduli by

$$
\beta = -\frac{\mu'\,\kappa}{2\mu}, \qquad \kappa_u = \frac{\kappa}{1-\alpha_B B},
$$

of order $\beta\approx-400$ for shallow sediments [@denolle2026unified; @clements2023seismic]. Whether
the *drained* modulus $\kappa$ or the *undrained* $\kappa_u$ (Biot coefficient $\alpha_B$, Skempton $B$)
applies is set by a Péclet number $\mathrm{Pe}=\omega L^2/c$ comparing the forcing rate to pore-pressure
diffusion — so a *rapid* rainfall pulse and a *slow* seasonal water-table change of equal magnitude
produce different $\delta v/v$ amplitudes. This is the mechanism by which the ambient field tracks
groundwater [@senss2006passive; @clements2018tracking]; our operational $\delta v/v = k_{sat}\,\Delta h$
is its linearization, with $k_{sat}$ identified as $-\beta\,\rho_w g$ (a calibratable acoustoelastic
coefficient), and only a *relative* level is recovered.

### Vadose zone — partial saturation and dynamic capillarity

Above the water table the effective confining pressure carries a **capillary stress**, and
$V_S=\sqrt{\mu_{\rm eff}/\rho}$ with $\mu_{\rm eff}$ set by [@shi2026agroseismology; @denolle2026unified]

$$
P_e(z,t) = \sigma'_g(z,t) + P_c^{\rm eq}(S_w) + \tau(S_w)\,\frac{\partial S_w}{\partial t},
$$

the gravitational overburden $\sigma'_g$, the *equilibrium* capillary pressure $P_c^{\rm eq}(S_w)$ set
by the soil's water-retention curve, and a *rate-dependent dynamic* capillary stress
$\tau(S_w)\,\partial_t S_w$ (Hassanizadeh–Gray non-equilibrium capillarity). Drying raises suction,
raises $P_e$, and stiffens the frame; wetting softens it. Shi et al. (2026) show three things that
matter here: dynamic capillarity is *essential* (a static-suction model underestimates the
evaporation-driven velocity rise by ~50%); the dynamic coefficient $\tau(S_w)$ differs between wetting
and drying, so the $\delta v/v$–saturation relation is **hysteretic** (path-dependent, not
single-valued); and capillary suction can raise $|\beta|$ by factors of 2–5. Our operational
$\delta v/v = S_\theta\,\Delta\theta$ is therefore the *local linearization* of a hysteretic,
retention-curve-controlled relation: $S_\theta$ is a **material property** — set by porosity, the
Hertz–Mindlin contact stiffness, and $P_c(S_w)$ — not a universal constant. Its material value
($\approx-1$ per unit $\theta$; the depth-diluted single-band value is $\approx-0.08$) applies to the
*inverted* profile.

### From velocity change to stress — the interface

Because both regimes act through $V_S=\sqrt{\mu_{\rm eff}/\rho}$, the depth-separated $\delta V_S/V_S$
profile converts to a *physical stress* change through the stress meter

$$
\Delta\sigma_{ij}(z,t) = \beta_{ij}(z)\,\frac{\delta V_S}{V_S}(z,t),
\qquad \mu(z)=\rho V_S^2,\quad \kappa(z)=\rho\!\left(V_P^2-\tfrac43 V_S^2\right),
$$

with $\beta_{ij}$ built from those layered moduli and $\mu'(z)$ [@denolle2026unified]. This is what
separates the approach from a fitted correlation: the conversion of $\delta v/v$ to soil moisture and
head is anchored to **measurable material properties** — density $\rho$, bulk and shear moduli
$\kappa,\mu$, the acoustoelastic $\beta$, and the retention curve $P_c(S_w)$ — which become the
borehole/lab **calibration targets** that carry the MVP's constant $S_\theta$ and $k_{sat}$ toward a
material-property-based, hysteresis-aware conversion. The banded measurements are inverted through the
Rayleigh kernels $\mathbf{G}$, $\ \mathbf{d}=\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{m}+\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, with
$\mathrm{cov}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon})$ marginalised over the processing ensemble
([`codameter`](https://github.com/Denolle-Lab/codameter)); splitting the profile at the water table
yields two hydrologic states from one measurement — a shallow **soil-moisture** change (vadose, dynamic
capillarity) and a deep **relative water-table** change (saturated, poroelastic) — each assimilated
into the matching hydrological state above ([Assimilation](04-assimilation.qmd)).
